metadata.summary: Design OpenAPI 3.1 specifications before implementation code. The spec drives mock generation, stubs, SDKs, and contract testing.

content.title: API-First Development
content.summary: Design the OpenAPI 3.1 specification before writing any implementation code. The spec is the contract: it drives mock server generation (Prism), server stub generation (OpenAPI Generator), client SDK creation, and contract testing (schemathesis/dredd). The spec must be kept in sync with the implementation via CI contract tests — a spec that lies is worse than no spec.

why.p: Code-first APIs produce inconsistent designs, undocumented edge cases, and breaking changes for consumers. API-first enables frontend and backend teams to work in parallel (frontend against the mock, backend against the spec), generates boilerplate automatically, and gives LLMs a machine-readable contract that dramatically reduces hallucinations during code generation.

list.item: Starting a new API — design the spec before writing any implementation code.
Frontend and backend developed in parallel — spec enables Prism mock server for frontend.
Generating server stubs, client SDKs, or TypeScript types from spec rather than writing by hand.
Onboarding an LLM to implement or test an API — full OpenAPI spec as context eliminates ambiguity.
Multiple consumers (web, mobile, third-party) will use the API — spec is the shared contract.
Internal one-off scripts or single-consumer CLI tools with no formal contract.
Rapidly prototyping throw-away spikes — write code first, extract spec after it stabilizes.
GraphQL APIs — GraphQL has its own SDL-first approach; OpenAPI does not map cleanly.
Simple CRUD with no external consumers — spec maintenance overhead exceeds benefit.

section.name: spec-design
section.p: Use OpenAPI 3.1 (not 3.0). Place all reusable schemas in components/schemas and reference them with $ref everywhere. Include example values in every schema field — Prism mock responses are generated from examples.

rule.statement: Use OpenAPI 3.1 (not 3.0). Place all reusable schemas in components/schemas and reference them with $ref everywhere. Include example values in every schema field — Prism mock responses are generated from examples.
rule.rationale: OpenAPI 3.1 aligns with JSON Schema draft 2020-12, enabling richer validation rules. $ref reuse ensures consistency: changing a schema updates it everywhere. Missing examples cause Prism mocks to return empty strings and zero values, making frontend development against the mock useless.

rule.statement: Mark every required field explicitly in the required: array. Use type: ['string', 'null'] (not nullable: true) for optional fields in OpenAPI 3.1. Use format hints (uuid, date-time, email) and validation constraints (minLength, pattern, enum).
rule.rationale: Explicit required arrays and type constraints give LLMs and code generators the information needed to produce correct validation code. nullable: true is OpenAPI 3.0 syntax that is deprecated in 3.1.

example.title: Proper schema with required fields and format hints
example.code: components: schemas: User: type: object required: [id, email, created_at] properties: id: type: string format: uuid example: "123e4567-e89b-12d3-a456-426614174000" email: type: string format: email example: "user@example.com" display_name: type: ['string', 'null'] # Optional, OpenAPI 3.1 style maxLength: 100 example: "Alice" created_at: type: string format: date-time example: "2026-01-15T12:00:00Z"

rule.statement: Use URL path versioning (/v1/, /v2/) for breaking changes. Maintain separate OpenAPI documents per major version. Set info.version with semantic versioning. Mark deprecated endpoints with deprecated: true before removal.
rule.rationale: URL path versioning is visible, cacheable, and easy to test. Separate spec files per major version keep each spec clean and allow tooling to target specific versions.

rule.statement: Run Spectral linting as a required CI check before any implementation begins. A spec that passes linting is the quality gate for proceeding to mock and implementation phases.
rule.rationale: Spectral catches missing operationIds (which break code generation), missing tags (which break SDK organization), and schema inconsistencies before any code is written.

antipattern.description: Modifying generated code to deviate from the spec, then not regenerating when the spec changes. The spec and implementation silently diverge.
antipattern.reason: Generated code that diverges from the spec makes the spec a lie. Add a hash of the spec file as a comment in generated files; if the spec changes, the hash mismatch signals that regeneration is required.

section.name: toolchain
section.p: The complete API-first workflow: spec → Spectral lint → Prism mock → OpenAPI Generator stubs → schemathesis contract tests. Tool roles and agent prompt patterns for each phase.

list.item: DESIGN — Write OpenAPI 3.1 spec (Stoplight Studio, Swagger Editor, or text editor).
VALIDATE — Spectral lint: spectral lint openapi.yaml --ruleset .spectral.yaml.
MOCK — Prism mock server: prism mock openapi.yaml --port 4010 --dynamic.
IMPLEMENT — Generate stubs: openapi-generator-cli generate -i openapi.yaml -g python-fastapi -o ./generated.
TEST — Contract tests: schemathesis run openapi.yaml --url http://localhost:8000.

rule.statement: Start Prism with --dynamic flag to generate random data from schema constraints when no explicit example is provided. Without --dynamic, missing examples produce empty responses.
rule.rationale: Frontend teams building against the mock need realistic data shapes. --dynamic generates values that match schema types, formats, and constraints even without explicit examples.

example.title: Starting Prism mock server with dynamic flag
example.code: # Start mock server (lint first) spectral lint openapi.yaml --ruleset .spectral.yaml prism mock openapi.yaml --port 4010 --dynamic & echo "Mock server running at http://localhost:4010" echo "Docs at http://localhost:4010/__docs"

rule.statement: Run schemathesis or dredd against the live implementation in CI. When a contract test fails, determine whether the spec or the implementation is correct — never auto-fix the spec to match a buggy implementation.
rule.rationale: Contract tests enforce that the implementation matches its documented contract. Automatically updating the spec to match implementation failures defeats the purpose of the spec as the source of truth.

rule.statement: When generating implementation code from spec, pass the full OpenAPI spec as context and instruct the LLM to use operationId values as function names. For APIs with 100+ endpoints, work on one tag group at a time to avoid context window overflow.
rule.rationale: operationId creates a traceable mapping from spec to code. Breaking large specs by tag group keeps the context manageable while maintaining the spec as the grounding source.

template.purpose: OpenAPI 3.1 spec skeleton with all required sections.
Spectral ruleset for org-standard spec linting.
